skill.name: testing-with-sandbox
skill.description: Test outbound email in development and staging without sending to real recipients. Use the Mailtrap Email Sandbox to capture messages, inspect HTML and headers, run spam and structure checks, and automate tests against a fake inbox.
skill.summary: Capture and inspect test emails in a sandbox inbox using Mailtrap's Email Sandbox API or SMTP.
skill.author: mailtrap

security_audit.summary: This is a pure documentation skill (a single SKILL.md) describing how to use Mailtrap's Email Sandbox for safe email testing. The 61 static findings are all false positives: the 'external_commands' detections are markdown code-fence backticks (not Ruby/shell execution), 'Hardcoded URL' entries are official Mailtrap documentation and API endpoint references, and 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' triggers come from the word 'token' in the context of API authentication. The 'Email sending capability' detections are the core intended function of this skill. No executable code, no prompt injection, and no malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Email sending capability references
medium_findings.description: The skill describes email sending to Mailtrap's sandbox test inboxes. This is the intended core functionality of an email testing skill, not a security concern. All destinations are Mailtrap's official sandbox endpoints (sandbox.smtp.mailtrap.io, sandbox.api.mailtrap.io) which capture mail without delivering to real recipients.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These references to email sending are clearly the documented purpose of the skill. Mailtrap sandbox explicitly does not deliver to real recipients.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation
low_findings.description: Multiple hardcoded URLs appear throughout the file. All are official Mailtrap documentation links (docs.mailtrap.io), GitHub repository links for Mailtrap SDKs (github.com/mailtrap), and Mailtrap API endpoints. None are exfiltration targets or suspicious domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs verified to be official Mailtrap domains. Documentation-only references pose no risk.

low_findings.title: False positive: backtick patterns detected as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged backtick characters in markdown formatting as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are inline code formatting backticks (e.g., `sandbox.smtp.mailtrap.io`) in markdown text, not actual command execution. There is no Ruby code or shell commands in this file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File contains only markdown documentation. Backticks are standard markdown inline code formatting.

low_findings.title: False positive: weak cryptographic algorithm detection
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' on lines 3 and 31. These lines contain no cryptographic code. Line 3 is the YAML frontmatter description and line 31 mentions API docs. The trigger likely comes from the word 'token' in authentication context, which is not cryptography implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No actual cryptographic code exists in this documentation file. The pattern is a false positive from keyword matching.

low_findings.title: False positive: system reconnaissance detection
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'system reconnaissance' on lines 41 and 70. These are documentation references to SMTP host/port/credentials configuration and template testing. No actual system enumeration or reconnaissance is performed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Content is documentation about SMTP configuration, not actual system reconnaissance behavior.

content.user_title: Test emails safely with Mailtrap Sandbox
content.value_statement: Developers need to verify outbound email without sending to real recipients. This skill guides Claude and Codex to configure and use the Mailtrap Email Sandbox for safe, automated email testing in development and CI environments.
actual_capabilities: Guide users to configure SMTP or HTTP API to capture outbound email in a sandbox inbox
Explain how to obtain and use the Mailtrap sandbox API token and inbox ID
Reference the correct API endpoints for listing sandboxes, messages, and sending test emails
Direct users to the right Mailtrap SDK repositories for Node.js, Python, PHP, Ruby, Java, .NET, and CLI
Help distinguish between sandbox (test) and transactional (live) email endpoints
Advise on best practices for avoiding production token misuse and common configuration mistakes
limitations: Does not provide framework-by-framework setup tutorials; refers to Mailtrap Integration tab
Cannot execute actual email sending or API calls; provides guidance only
Does not cover live email sending; use the sending-emails skill for production ESP integration
Requires users to supply their own Mailtrap account, sandbox token, and inbox ID

use_cases.title: Set up email testing in a CI pipeline
use_cases.description: A backend developer wants automated tests to verify that their application sends correct email content. Use this skill to configure SMTP settings pointing to the Mailtrap sandbox and assert against captured messages.

use_cases.title: Debug email rendering in development
use_cases.description: A frontend developer needs to inspect HTML email output during template development. This skill helps them capture emails in a sandbox inbox and review headers, bodies, and spam scores.

use_cases.title: Integrate email testing into staging environment
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer configuring a staging environment wants to ensure no real emails are sent. This skill provides the SMTP host, port, and token scope guidance needed to safely redirect all outbound mail.

prompt_templates.title: Configure SMTP for sandbox testing
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to configure my application to send all outbound email to a Mailtrap sandbox inbox instead of real recipients. What SMTP host, port, and credentials should I use?
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial setup of email capture in development

prompt_templates.title: Send a test email via the Sandbox API
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to send a test email to my Mailtrap sandbox inbox using the HTTP API. I have my inbox ID and a sandbox API token ready.

prompt_templates.title: Fetch and assert on captured messages
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to write a test that fetches messages from my Mailtrap sandbox inbox and verifies the subject line and HTML body. What API endpoint should I call?
prompt_templates.scenario: Writing automated assertions against captured email

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot sandbox vs transactional confusion
prompt_templates.prompt: My emails are going to real recipients instead of the sandbox. I think I am using the wrong endpoint. How do I make sure I am using the Mailtrap testing API and not the live sending API?

output_examples.input: What SMTP settings should I use to send test email to a Mailtrap sandbox inbox?
output: Username and Password: Use the credentials from the Integration tab of your specific sandbox inbox in the Mailtrap UI
Important: Never use sandbox credentials in production. Messages captured here are not delivered to real recipients.

output_examples.input: How do I fetch a list of messages from my sandbox inbox via the API?
output: Note: Resolve account_id at runtime via GET https://mailtrap.io/api/accounts
Use a token with Testing or Sandbox scope, not a live sending token.

output_examples.input: What is the difference between the Mailtrap sandbox API and the live sending API?
output: Sandbox API (sandbox.api.mailtrap.io): Captures email in a test inbox; messages are never delivered to real recipients
Live sending API (send.api.mailtrap.io): Delivers email to actual recipients via your production ESP
Tokens are separate: $MAILTRAP_SANDBOX_API_TOKEN for testing, $MAILTRAP_API_TOKEN for live sending
Always verify you are using the correct endpoint and token scope for your environment.

best_practices: Always use a dedicated sandbox-scoped token ($MAILTRAP_SANDBOX_API_TOKEN) for testing, never reuse a live sending token
Read the SDK README for current sandbox mode options and constructor flags before generating integration code
Verify that the inbox ID is supplied in all API calls to associate messages with the correct test inbox
anti_patterns: Using the live transactional API token or endpoint in a development or testing environment
Assuming sandbox email will be delivered to real recipients instead of captured in the test inbox
Hardcoding the inbox ID or API token in source code instead of using environment variables

faq.question: Does the Mailtrap sandbox deliver email to real recipients?
faq.answer: No. Email sent to a sandbox inbox is captured and stored for inspection. It is never delivered to real recipients.

faq.question: Can I use the same API token for sandbox and live sending?
faq.answer: No. The sandbox requires a separate token with Testing or Sandbox scope ($MAILTRAP_SANDBOX_API_TOKEN). Do not reuse the live sending token.

faq.question: How do I find my inbox ID?
faq.answer: The inbox ID is visible in the Mailtrap UI URL and in the Integration tab of your sandbox inbox. It is required for API operations.

faq.question: What is the difference between sandbox.smtp.mailtrap.io and send.api.mailtrap.io?
faq.answer: sandbox.smtp.mailtrap.io and sandbox.api.mailtrap.io are for testing and capture. send.api.mailtrap.io is the live sending API that delivers to real recipients.

faq.question: Which SDKs support Mailtrap sandbox mode?
faq.answer: Official Mailtrap SDKs for Node.js, Python, PHP, Ruby, Java, .NET, and CLI all support sandbox or test inbox operations. See the respective GitHub repositories for the latest flags and options.

faq.question: Can I automate assertions on captured email content?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the Mailtrap Sandbox API to list and fetch messages from your inbox, then assert on subject, HTML body, headers, and spam score in your test suite or CI pipeline.
